description: A stunning small tree with magenta-pink flowers that bloom before the leaves emerge in spring. One of the earliest flowering native trees, providing critical early nectar.
An iconic prairie wildflower with rosy-purple petals surrounding a spiky orange cone. Beloved by pollinators in bloom and by goldfinches and other seed-eaters in fall and winter.
A multi-season native shrub with white spring flowers, edible blue-purple berries in summer, and brilliant orange-red fall color. One of the highest-value native shrubs for birds.
Cheerful golden-yellow flowers with dark centers. A tough, drought-tolerant wildflower native to prairies and open woods. Seed heads feed finches through winter.
A vigorous, native climbing vine with spectacular orange-red tubular flowers. The premier hummingbird magnet in the eastern U.S. Flowers from midsummer to fall.
A loose, arching shrub spectacular in fall when clusters of vivid magenta-purple berries line the stems. Provides essential fall food for over 40 bird species.
One of eastern North America's most beloved native trees. White or pink bracts in spring, red berries in fall. Berries are a critical high-fat food for migrating birds.
A shaggy-headed native perennial with lavender-pink flowers. Aromatic foliage deters deer. Hummingbirds are devoted visitors; seed heads feed sparrows in winter.
Nodding red-and-yellow spurred flowers perfectly shaped for hummingbirds. One of the earliest nectar sources of spring. Thrives in woodland edges and rocky slopes.
A tall, airy native warm-season grass with an architectural quality. Seed heads persist through winter, feeding juncos, sparrows, and finches. Also provides nesting cover.
A majestic, fast-growing native oak with brilliant red fall color. Studies show that oaks support more caterpillar species than any other native tree — making them the #1 bird-supporting plant.
A fragrant native shrub of moist woodlands. Tiny yellow flowers in early spring before any leaves emerge. Scarlet berries in fall are a critical food for migrating warblers and thrushes.
A native vine with distinctive 5-leaflet leaves turning brilliant scarlet in fall. Small dark berries feed woodpeckers, thrushes, and waxwings.

A fast-growing native shrub with large flat-topped clusters of white flowers and dark purple berries. One of the most productive native fruit shrubs for birds — over 50 species recorded feeding on the berries.
Lavender-pink blooms attract hummingbirds and a wealth of native bees. The seed heads are sought by American Goldfinches and other finches in fall.
